records.ety: [OE. crepen, creopen, AS. crepan; akin to D. kruipen, G. kriechen, Icel. krjupa, Sw. krypa, Dan. krybe. Cf. Cripple, Crouch.]
records.morph: [imp. Crept (krpt) (Crope (krōp), Obs.); p. p. Crept; p. pr. & vb. n. Creeping.]

defs: To move along the ground, or on any other surface, on the belly, as a worm or reptile; to move as a child on the hands and knees; to crawl.
quotes: Ye that walk The earth, and stately tread, or lowly creep.

defs: To move slowly, feebly, or timorously, as from unwillingness, fear, or weakness.
quotes: The whining schoolboy . . . creeping, like snail, Unwillingly to school.
Like a guilty thing, I creep.

defs: To move in a stealthy or secret manner; to move imperceptibly or clandestinely; to steal in; to insinuate itself or one's self; as, age creeps upon us.
quotes: The sophistry which creeps into most of the books of argument.
Of this sort are they which creep into houses, and lead captive silly women.

defs: To slip, or to become slightly displaced; as, the collodion on a negative, or a coat of varnish, may creep in drying; the quicksilver on a mirror may creep.
To move or behave with servility or exaggerated humility; to fawn; as, a creeping sycophant.
quotes: To come as humbly as they used to creep.

defs: To grow, as a vine, clinging to the ground or to some other support by means of roots or rootlets, or by tendrils, along its length.
To have a sensation as of insects creeping on the skin of the body; to crawl; as, the sight made my flesh creep. See Crawl, v. i., 4.
To drag in deep water with creepers, as for recovering a submarine cable.
The act or process of creeping.
A distressing sensation, or sound, like that occasioned by the creeping of insects.
quotes: Out of the stillness, with gathering creep, Like rising wind in leaves.

defs: A slow rising of the floor of a gallery, occasioned by the pressure of incumbent strata upon the pillars or sides; a gradual movement of mining ground.
